coredata.dc:title: Nanocomposite pectin fibers incorporating folic acid-decorated carbon quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Zirak Hassan Kiadeh, Shahrzad
Ghaee, Azadeh
Pishbin, Fatemehsadat
Nourmohammadi, Jhamak
Farokhi, Mehdi
coredata.dc:description: Pectin has recently attracted increasing attention as an alternative biomaterial commonly used in biomedical and pharmaceutical fields. It shows several promising properties, including good biocompatibility, health benefits, nontoxicity, and biodegradation. In this research, novel nanocomposite fibers composed of folic acid-decorated carbon dots (CDs) in pectin/PEO matrix were fabricated using the electrospinning technique, which was never reported previously. Nitrogen-doped and nitrogen, sulfur-doped CDs were synthesized with average diameters of 2.74 nm and 2.17 nm using the one-step hydrothermal method, studied regarding their physicochemical, optical, and biocompatibility properties. The relative Quantum yields of N-CDs and N, S doped CDs were measured to be 54.7 % and 30.2 %, respectively. Nanocomposite fibers containing CDs were prepared, and their morphology, physicochemical properties, conductivity, drug release behavior, and cell viability were characterized. The results indicated that CDs improve fibrous scaffolds' tensile strength from 13.74 to 35.22 MPa while maintaining comparable extensibility. Furthermore, by incorporation of CDs in the prepared fibers conductivity enhanced from 8.69 × 10−9 S·m−1 to 1.36 × 10−4 S·m−1. The nanocomposite fibrous scaffold was also biocompatible with controlled drug release over 212 h, potentially promising tissue regeneration.

head.ce:title: Nanocomposite pectin fibers incorporating folic acid-decorated carbon quantum dots

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Pectin has recently attracted increasing attention as an alternative biomaterial commonly used in biomedical and pharmaceutical fields. It shows several promising properties, including good biocompatibility, health benefits, nontoxicity, and biodegradation. In this research, novel nanocomposite fibers composed of folic acid-decorated carbon dots (CDs) in pectin/PEO matrix were fabricated using the electrospinning technique, which was never reported previously. Nitrogen-doped and nitrogen, sulfur-doped CDs were synthesized with average diameters of 2.74 nm and 2.17 nm using the one-step hydrothermal method, studied regarding their physicochemical, optical, and biocompatibility properties. The relative Quantum yields of N-CDs and N, S doped CDs were measured to be 54.7 % and 30.2 %, respectively. Nanocomposite fibers containing CDs were prepared, and their morphology, physicochemical properties, conductivity, drug release behavior, and cell viability were characterized. The results indicated that CDs improve fibrous scaffolds' tensile strength from 13.74 to 35.22 MPa while maintaining comparable extensibility. Furthermore, by incorporation of CDs in the prepared fibers conductivity enhanced from 8.69 × 10−9 S·m−1 to 1.36 × 10−4 S·m−1. The nanocomposite fibrous scaffold was also biocompatible with controlled drug release over 212 h, potentially promising tissue regeneration.

ce:section.ce:para: Biopolymers are a class of macromolecules that can be obtained from natural sources or synthesized either biologically or chemically using the living organisms [1]. The main advantages of biopolymers over synthetic polymers are non-toxicity, biodegradability, and easy availability. In addition, they also well fit in environmentally friendly and adjustable materials, promoting their use for biomedical applications [2,3]. Polysaccharides are an abundant class of biopolymers in the environment containing long chains of carbohydrates, effectively used for the preparation of various biomaterials either directly or indirectly [3,4]. Pectin is a natural hetero-polysaccharide. It mainly comprises smooth homogalacturonan (HG) domains and hairy rhamnogalacturonan (RG) domains. RG domains contain (1, 4)-α-linked galacturonic acid, which can be methyl esterified in the C-6 position or non-esterified [5–7]. Pectin can be extracted from terrestrial plant tissue and waste biomass such as citrus peel and apple pomace, which are by-products of juice manufacturing [1,3,8]. This anionic biopolymer can regulate inflammation and improve cell signaling, adhesion, proliferation, and differentiation due to its naturally esterified galacturonic acid units [9–15]. Biopolymer-based fibers have intrinsic properties such as high surface area and porosity, helping them mimic the structure of extracellular matrices (ECMs) [16] and enhance interactions with cells and tissues [17]. They are also better mobile carriers for drugs and have good filtration and absorption abilities. Electrospinning is one of the most uncomplicated and economical techniques to fabricate nanofibers from polymeric solutions by applying a high voltage at a micro or nanoscale [4,18–20].

It is proved that the electrospinning of charged natural polysaccharides such as chitosan, alginate, pectin, et cetera on their own is challenging, emphasizing the necessity of applying co-polymers, additives, and suitable solvents. The reasons are complex molecular chain entanglements in solution and various rheological properties of such polymers [4,21]. In the case of Pectin, Pectin with PEO (polyethylene oxide) as a co-polymer together with Triton X-100 as an additive, dispersed in an aqueous mixture, with or without DMSO, yielded electrospun fibers that were successfully uniform and smooth [5,17,22]. Various extraction sources also alter the morphologies, fiber diameters, and tensile characteristics of electrospun pectin fibers due to different degrees of pectin's esterification [23]. Moreover, the properties of the polysaccharides, such as their low mechanical resistance, can be improved using the incorporation of reinforcing additives into matrices [1,24]. Carbon dots (CDs) are zero-dimensional Nano-structures with <10 nm diameter, mainly consisting of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms [25,26]. Their unique properties include facile surface modification [25], antiviral activity [27–29], chemical and photo-stability [30], excellent biocompatibility, and widely tunable optical and electrical properties [26]. So, they have been utilized widely for bio-imaging and nanomedicine [31,32], photocatalysis [33,34], sensing [35,36], and electret-transducing [37]. Recently, there has been an increasing interest in functionalizing fiber-based materials with CDs for various purposes. Alam et al. incorporated CDs into a polymeric matrix (polyacrylonitrile/polyacrylic acid) as a strategy to overcome aggregation and produced electrospun optically transparent and fluorescent mats [25]. In another study, Zuo et al. reported the fabrication of UV-protective cotton fabric containing nitrogen and boron co-doped CDs [38]. Nie et al. focused on the antibacterial activity of polyacrylonitrile (PAN) nanofibers containing CDs through photodynamic inactivation. They suggested that the fluorescent nanofibers had broad antibacterial efficacy against Gram-negative (Escherichia coli and Pseudomonas aeruginosa) and Gram-Positive (Bacillus subtilis) bacteria upon visible light (λ ≥ 420 nm) exposition [26]. In addition to that, Cortim et al. incorporated CDs into PCL/silk electrospun yarns having superior mechanical properties and porosity compared to nanofibers mat [25]. On the other hand, CDs' photoluminescent behavior (PL) is affected by several factors such as functional groups on the CDs' surfaces, their size, interband transitions, and surface defect states [39], so the application of un-doped CDs in bioanalysis is limited. Due to the intra-molecular ground-state dimer complex or energy transfer between the adjacent CDs, they might have self-quenching [40].

In this article, the folic acid-functionalized CDs were incorporated into the electrospun pectin/PEO nanofibrous mat for the first time with enhanced mechanical properties and conductivity as well as controlled drug release. The nitrogen-doped (N-doped) and nitrogen, sulfur-doped (N, S-doped) CDs were synthesized using the one-pot hydrothermal method, with functional groups of Folic Acids on their surfaces. CDs were investigated and compared with each other by TEM, FTIR, UV/Visible spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, and the electric conductivity measurement. The effects of CDs' addition into polymeric fibers were evaluated regarding the morphology, mechanical properties, drug release behavior, and conductivity. The results indicated that the biocompatible electrospun pectin fibers composed of CDs are promising candidates as drug delivery systems as well as scaffolds. The detailed scheme of preparation is provided in Fig. 1 .

Citrus pectin (galacturonic acid ≥ 74.0 %), Poly ethylene oxide (PEO) (Molecular weight (Mw) of 4000 kDa), calcium chloride dehydrate (CaCl2, Ca2+ ion as a crosslinker), Citric acid, thiourea, sodium hydroxide (NaOH), Folic acid (≥97 % purity), phosphate buffered saline (PBS), and [3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide] (MTT) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, USA. Triton X-100 and glacial acetic acid were supplied by Emd, USA and Acros, USA, respectively. Ethanol (C2H5OH, 99 %), glutaraldehyde (GLU) and hydrochloric acid (HCl, 36.5–38.0 % (w/w) purity) were obtained from Merck, Germany. Human glioblastoma cell line (U-373) was obtained from the National Cell Bank (Iran). All reagents were used as received without further purification and all aqueous solutions were prepared with double distilled water.

In this study, CDs were synthesized via one-pot hydrothermal carbonization. This method was a facile procedure to incorporate folic acid and dope heteroatoms such as nitrogen and sulfur atoms into CDs, consequently improving the photoluminescence properties of the nanoparticles [41]. Initially, 2 g of citric acid as a carbon source was dissolved in 20 mL of deionized water (DI), forming a homogenous solution to produce un-doped CDs (CIT) used only to confirm Folic acid existence. In the case of Folic acid-decorated N-doped CDs (FACIT), 0.02 g Folic acid also was added to the citric acid solution. Moreover, to synthesize Folic acid-decorated N, S-doped CDs (FACITUR), the initial yellow solution was composed of 1 g citric acid, 1 g thiourea (sulfur source), and 0.02 g Folic acid. The detailed information about initial solutions to synthesize CDs was summarized in Table 1 .

Afterward, all initial homogenous solutions were transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. Then, it was pyrolyzed in the oven at 200 °C for 6 h. The resulting brown-red solutions were cooled to room temperature, centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 20 min, and sequentially dialyzed against DI water using dialysis membrane under the stirring condition to remove black sediments and other large particles. Then, the pH of CDs solutions was neutralized to 7.0 with NaOH solution and preserved in the dark place at 4 °C for further assessments. The freeze-drying technique also was applied to obtain a black-brown powder needed in some analysis.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Philips CM-300) was applied to investigate the size and morphology of the CDs. High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were obtained for all tested samples using a FEI Tecnai G2 F20 microscope (USA). Sonicated CDs solutions in distilled water were prepared for dynamic light scattering (DLS) and Zeta potential testing to determine the size distribution as well as surface charge of them (SZ-100z, Horiba Ltd., UK).

The conductivity of the aqueous CDs suspensions was measured using a conductometer (pH Conductivity Meter, WANT Balance instrument Co, China). The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, PerkinElmer) spectra of each sample mixed with KBr were recorded over the wavenumber range of 400–4000 cm−1 to qualify the existing functional groups of CDs. Moreover, the prepared CDs' UV–Vis absorbance and photoluminescence (PL) spectra were characterized by Cary 5000 ultraviolet-visible-near infrared spectrometer and Cary Eclipse Varian fluorescence spectrofluorometer, respectively. Quantum yield (QY) of CDs was calculated by plotting the absorbance values and integrated fluorescence intensities of Rhodamine B as a reference (QY = 89 % in ethanol, λex = 495 nm) and samples at the same wavelength. The gradients of the linear fits of these lines were input into the following formula:
QY S

where the subscript “R” refers to the reference Rhodamine B, while “S” refers to the CDs sample; “G” is the gradient of the linear fit of absorbance against integrated fluorescence intensity; “η” is the refractive index of the solvent which for Rhodamine B was ethanol and for carbon dots was water [42]. FACIT and FACITUR were characterized by X-ray diffraction analysis (XRD) analysis using a PHILIPS X'Pert MPD diffractometer (Netherlands) equipped with Cu rotating anode X-ray source (λ = 1.54 Å). Measurements were recorded in reflection mode in the range of 0–100°.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell culture and cellular viability assay of CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The viability of the human glioblastoma cell line (U-373) treated with the aqueous solution of Folic acid-decorated CDs was examined by MTT assay. The cells were cultured in Dulbecco's Modification of Eagles Medium complemented with 10 % (v/v) fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 1 % (v/v) antibiotic (100 μg mL−1 of streptomycin and 100 U mL−1 of penicillin) in a humidified incubator with 5 % CO2-containing balanced air at 37 °C. Briefly, 100 μL of cells (1 × 104 cells per well) was seeded in each well of a 96-well plate. Afterward, the culture medium was refreshed with 100 μL medium containing varying concentrations of prepared CDs (from 0 to 400
μg mL
) for 24-hour incubation. Subsequently, MTT solution (10 μL, 5
mg mL
) was added to each well after the culture medium was removed, then cells were incubated for another 4 h. After that, the supernatant was discarded, and the formazan was dissolved by adding DMSO to each well (150 μL). The formazan crystals formed by mitochondria activity were dissolved in Isopropanol for 15 min. Finally, the optical density (OD) of solutions related to the concentrations of dissolved salts in each well was measured at a wavelength of 570 nm using an ELISA microplate reader (Stat Fax-2100, USA), relative cell viabilities were determined. Cells not treated with CDs were used as controls. This assay was repeated three times, and the percentage of cell viability was calculated by the below equation:
(1)
Cell viability % =
Sample '
s
average
OD
Control '
s
average
OD
× 100

Pectin/PEO fibers (PECO) were electrospun based on our previously reported study [22]. However, to prepare CDs-loaded Pectin/PEO fibers, 1 wt% Triton X-100 and 0.1 g PEO were added to 10 mL of CDs suspensions containing approximately 0.4 g CDs (4 wt%), then the mixture was stirred on a beaker for 6–8 h. After completely dissolving PEO, pectin and another 1 wt% Triton X-100 were mixed in the solution. In this way, there are 0.3 g pectin and 0.1 g PEO in 10 mL sample solutions. The pectin ratio to PEO was 3:1 (w/w) in all sample solutions. Each 5 mL syringe loaded with the resultant homogenous solution was capped with a 20-gauge stainless steel needle. The distance between the syringe needle tip and collector and feed rate was adjusted to 25 cm and 1 mL h−1. The detailed information about electrospinning solutions and parameters are provided in Table 2 .

To crosslink composite fibers, two crosslinking steps were carried out. In the first covalent crosslinking step, the electrospun fibers were placed in a sealed desiccator and exposed to glutaraldehyde vapor and HCl (with a ratio of 9:1) for 1 h at 60 °C [43]. Secondly, the supplementary ionic crosslinking of fibers were conducted by Ca2+ following the modified protocol based on the studies reported previously [17,44,45]. Briefly, covalent-crosslinked fibers were initially immersed in absolute ethanol for 5 min, then pre-crosslinked in ethanol (95 %) containing CaCl2 (2 wt%) for 20 min. Then, an aqueous solution of CaCl2 (40 wt%) was used to crosslink fibers completely. Finally, the crosslinked fibers were washed with glycine solution, excess DI, and phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) to eliminate unreacted glutaraldehyde and CaCl2. The pectin/PEO fibers containing FACIT were named as PECOCIT and the fibers with FACITUR CDs were introduced as PECOCITUR in Table 2.

The microstructure of fibers was imaged by environmental scanning electron microscope (ESEM, FEI ESEM Quanta200) to assess their morphology and the cells' distribution and adhesion on them. The average diameters and dispersity of fibers were evaluated using ImageJ software (National Institutes of Health). In this way, three SEM micrographs were captured for each group of samples, and the diameters of 50 fibers randomly selected in each SEM image were measured to calculate the statistical average diameter of each sample. Also, oxygen, nitrogen, and sulfur atoms in fibers were detected by energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX, FEI ESEM Quanta200) to confirm the existence of FACITs or FACITURs.

Attenuated Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (ATR-FTIR, PerkinElmer, Norwalk, CT) was used to study chemical bonds and functional groups of pectin-based mats in the range of 650–4000
cm
− 1
.

A Keithley model 2361 multimeter (Keithley Instruments, USA) was applied to determine the electrical conductivity of the fibers at room temperature. Three similar samples were tested from each dried electrospun sheet, and the thickness of each sheet was measured. Resistance (R) of fibers was calculated by measuring current while applying a voltage range from −10 V to 10 V with 0.2 V steps into both sides of the sample using circular electrodes. Eq. (2) was used to calculate the resistivity (R) of scaffolds as follows:
(2)

“V” and “I” are the applied voltage and current. Also, the electrical conductivity (σ) was calculated by Eq. (3):

Which “L” and “A” are the distance between two electrodes and the area of the electrodes, respectively [43,46,47].

The tensile mechanical properties of the fibers were ascertained using a mechanical testing machine equipped with a data acquisition system (5566, Instron, USA), and their mechanical properties were compared. Each crosslinked mat was cut into 40 mm × 5 mm rectangular specimens and mounted onto the clamps of the mechanical testing machine. After that, the rectangular-shaped mats were subjected to static tensile at a tensile speed of 10 [48]. At least five samples were mechanically tested for each condition. Young's modulus (E), ultimate tensile strength (σ), and ultimate tensile strain at break (ε) were calculated from the load (N) vs. strain curves using specific weights. The specific strength (S. σ) was obtained using the Eq. (4) [49]:

To study the release rate of Folic acid from fibers containing FA-decorated CDs, 2 mm2 (weighed approximately 2 mg) of each sample were put into a dialysis membrane placed in 4 mL PBS solution (pH = 7.4, 1 M). The release medium slowly had been shaken by an incubator-shaker at 37 °C. The specific volumes of medium were drawn out from outside the dialysis membrane at determined time intervals which were compensated with a fresh release medium of the same volume. Afterward, the concentration of folic acid in each aliquot was detected via UV–Visible absorption spectroscopy (Epoch Nanodrop) and a folic acid calibration curve.

The drug concentrations were corrected through Eq. (6) published by Klimundová et al. [50]:

The swelling index was accounted as water uptake degree performed by incubating the fibers (repeated three times for each sample group) in PBS with the pH of 7.4 at 37 °C. Initially, each dried sample was accurately cut into 10 × 10 mm2, and then they were immersed in PBS until the swelling equilibrium was reached. Ultimately, after gently removing the excess surface water of samples using filter paper, they were weighed again. The SI of the mats was calculated according to the following equation:
(7)

where Wt is the weight of each swollen sample and W0 is the initial weight of each sample.

The in vitro cytotoxicity of PECO, PECOCIT, and PECOTUR was determined through indirect MTT assay for 1, 5, 7, and 10 days intervals. The extracts of fibers were prepared according to ISO 10993-5 standard. The general protocol was mentioned in Section 2.3.1. Briefly, after 24-hour U-373 cell seeding, the extracts were added to each well with a fresh complete culture medium. At the end of each period, the supernatant was removed, and the cells were rinsed with PBS. Then, 100 μl of DMEM containing 10 μl of sterile-filtered MTT solution was added to each well incubated for another 4 h in dark condition. The optical density of Isopropanol solutions was recorded at a wavelength of 570 nm (with ELISA reader apparatus). The control group includes the cells cultured in DMEM medium without adding fibers extracts. Each group was tested in triplicate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular morphology on the composite fibers
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the cellular morphology and attachment of U-373 cells on the fibrous mats, 2 × 105 cells were seeded and incubated in a 24-well cell culture plate for three days. The culture medium was DMEM supplemented with 10 % FBS, 100 μg/mL streptomycin, and 100 IU/mL penicillin incubated at 37 °C and 5 % CO2. On day 3, the culture medium was removed entirely, and the buffer containing 4 % glutaraldehyde was poured on the attached cells for 90 min to fix them. Subsequently, fibers were washed with PBS to remove residual glutaraldehyde and immersed in dehydrated in graded aqueous ethanol (30, 50, 70, 90, and 100 %) for 10 min. Finally, the scaffolds were sputter-coated with gold for further ESEM imaging.

In this study, all quantitative data were reported as means ± standard deviation (SD) for n ≥ 3. The statistical analysis to determine statistically significant differences between the groups was conducted by two-way analysis of variance (ANOVA). A confidence level of 95 % (p < 0.05) was considered to indicate statistical significance.

Water-soluble, highly stable aqueous N-CDs and N, S-CDs suspensions were synthesized using one-step carbonization of citric acid along with only folic acid (FACIT) or folic acid and thiourea (FACITUR), respectively. The amount of FA was based on literature [51] which improves fluorescence property [51,52] as well as cell growth and proliferation of the CDs. The solution color alters from yellow to reddish-brown during the hydrothermal process, indicating the successful synthesis of FA-decorated CDs.

The morphology and particle size of FACIT and FACITUR were characterized by TEM shown in Fig. 2 . It was revealed that the CDs were prepared as quite spherical and monodisperse particles, not agglomerated. The TEM characterization demonstrated that the diameters of FACITs and FACITURs statistically were distributed in the ranges of 2–5 nm and 1–4 nm, respectively. The average diameter of 50 FACITs was calculated 2.74 ± 0.60 nm, while the mean size of 50 FACITUR was 2.17 ± 0.36 nm. High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images of FACIT suggest that it possesses multiple types of domains. The d-spacing obtained by HRTEM (shown in Fig. 2) are confirmed by Powder XRD spectra of CDs. Fig. 3 shows the XRD pattern of the synthesized folic acid-decorated CDs. In the case of FACITUR, the observed broad diffraction peak centered at 2θ = 27.0°, suggesting the presence of disordered carbon atoms and the existence of a graphitic-like structure [(002) lattice planes] [53,54]. However, It seems that FACIT has a poly crystallite structure [55,56]. The distribution of CDs diameters also measured by DLS in colloidal aqueous dispersion revealed a hydrodynamic diameter of 4.6 ± 0.2 nm and 2.9 ± 2.2 nm for FACIT and FACITUR. The zeta potentials of FACIT and FACITUR were 4 and −2.7 mV, respectively. The zeta potential of FACITUR was lower than the FACIT CDs due to the presence of more negatively charged carboxyl groups on the CDs' surface by folic acid modification. The conductivity of aqueous FACIT suspension was 3.95 μS; meanwhile, the conductivity of FACITUR suspension was enhanced to 77.3 μS.

The FTIR spectra of modified CDs depict their surface functional groups compared to free FA molecules, shown in Fig. 2. In the FACIT spectrum, there is a broad peak from 2400 to 3650 cm−1, including the peaks related to OH, NH (3200–3500 cm−1), and CH of aliphatic groups (2889–2952 cm−1) vibrations which display the successful modification of quantum dots with folic acid [51]. This curve also shows a broad peak at 1722 cm−1 could result from CO vibrations of carboxyl and acetyl groups [57,58]. The peaks that existed around 1640 and 1400 cm−1 represent the presence of CO/NH and COOH, respectively, relating to folic acid [59].

In the FACITUR spectrum, the peaks at 1049, 1403, and 1553 cm−1 emerge from COC, CN, and CC vibrations, respectively [57,60]. The broad absorption band at 3200–3500 cm−1 corresponds to NH and OH stretching vibrations. SC bond causes a peak at 1191 cm−1 which its stretching mode appears in the range of 1050–1400 cm−1. CO and CN absorption bands also might exist in the same region [61]. Additionally, the peak at 1667 cm−1 confirms the existence of CO bonds in the NHCO groups, consequently verifying the formation process of the FACITURs [51,62].

Crucially, the peaks at around 1400 cm−1, 1500 cm−1, and 1600 cm−1, could correspond to the presence of aromatic systems in folic acid chemical structures. In addition, some broad peaks in the 2750–3500 cm−1 also seem to be related to the NH and NH2 stretching vibrations found in the secondary and primary amines of folic acid. So the FTIR spectra show many standard features between the spectra of FACIT, FACITUR, and folic acid molecules, suggesting that the structures of folic acid largely remain intact in FA-decorated CDs [51,52,62].

The optical properties of FA-decorated CDs were studied by UV–Vis and fluorescence spectroscopies depicted in Figs. 2 and 4 . The optical properties of aqueous folic acid, CIT, FACIT, and FACITUR solutions, assessed by the UV–Vis absorption method, were used to verify The FTIR results. The sample of CIT was prepared for a detailed comparison.

CDs usually have prominent optical absorbance in the ultraviolet region extending to the visible light. The narrow band of 200–300 nm in carbon dots spectra is accounted for π–π* transition. The peaks at 230 and 252 nm in FACITUR and FACIT spectra are assigned to π–π* transition of CC and CN (Aromatic sp2 domain). This peak also was observed at 230 nm in CIT [51,63–65]. There is another peak related to n–π* transition of the carbon lattice edge, e.g., from the CO species [40,66] appeared at 345 nm in FACIT spectrum and at 332 nm in FACITUR spectrum. However, recent studies demonstrate that these absorption bands can originate from electronic transitions in organic fluorophores [67,68] confirming the existence of FA molecules [41,51,62].

Two characteristic peaks of Folic acid are observed at 283 and 361 nm in their UV–Vis spectrum. The absorption peak at 361 nm is a permanent energy band of the pterin ring in the FA structure, so it is often used as an essential index of FA molecule structure [52,69]. As shown in Fig. 2, the characteristic peak of FA at around 360 nm can be seen clearly in all samples except CIT, indicating that the FA molecules also keep their characteristics in the FA-decorated CDs.

The photoluminescence (PL) behavior of the reddish-brown solutions of synthesized CDs was evaluated in different wavelengths shown in Fig. 4. From PL spectra of FACIT and FACITUR, there are the excitonic absorption bands at 495 and 515 nm to obtain the most substantial emission peaks at 511 and 524 nm, respectively.

The corresponding PL emission peak does not display impressive shifts when CDs are excited from 480 to 500 nm (Fig. 4 (b, c)). Therefore, the FA-decorated CDs aqueous solutions show excitation-independent PL behaviors. The results may result from the particle size distribution and surface state of FACITs and FACITURs [62,70]. The surface state is equivalent to a molecular state, while the size effect relates to quantum dimensions. Both of them are contributed to the complexity of the excited states of CDs [71]. Using Rhodamine B in ethanol as a reference (QY = 89 %, λex = 495 nm), the relative QY of N-CDs was measured to be 54.7 % at λex/λem of 495/515–700 nm. N, S doped CDs had a QY of 30.2 %.
As described above, the TEM, FTIR, UV–Vis, and fluorescence spectroscopy analysis together indicated the successful preparation of FA-decorated CDs.

U-373 cells viability was investigated through MTT assay followed by cell culture and exposure against four different concentrations of FACITs and FACITURs in the ranges of 50–400 μg mL−1 for 24 h. According to MTT results of FACITs (Fig. 2), although it is observed that the viability of U-373 was lower than the control sample, the values of >90 % signified no cytotoxicity caused by 50, 100, and 200 μg mL−1 FACITs. However, with a further increase in the concentration of FACITs up to 400 μg mL−1, cytotoxicity was noticed.

Meanwhile, the survival rate of U-373 remained over 90 % even when cultured with a considerable concentration of 400 μg mL−1 FACITURs. When the concentration of FACITURs increased up to 200 μg mL−1, no statistically significant changes were observed between control and treated samples concluded as no cytotoxicity. Conclusively, based on these findings, it could be conceived that the FACITUR exhibited lower biological toxicity than FACIT. Moreover, FACITUR and FACIT solutions with concentrations of <400 and 200 μg mL−1, respectively, have high biocompatibility and could be used in further steps of nanofibrous scaffold fabrication.

The diameter distribution curves and ESEM/Edax images of pectin fibers containing FACITs or FACITURs are shown in Fig. 6. As observed in Fig. 5 , the mean fiber diameters of PECO, PECOCIT, and PECOTUR were 513.3 ± 8.1 nm, 516.1 ± 7.3 nm, 772.5 ± 13.4 nm, respectively. The difference between the average diameters of PECO and PECOCIT is not statistically significant. The addition of CDs in electrospinning solutions had two main effects. It raises the electrical conductivity and viscosity of the solutions. Higher conductivity leads to a decrease in the fibers' mean diameter while enhanced viscosity increase electrospun fibers' diameter, as the same voltage was used, respectively [38,64,72]. The changes in viscosity affect more effective than conductivity. The reason would be that the process voltage reduced as conductivity increased. The presence of nitrogen and oxygen in PECOCIT and sulfur, nitrogen, and oxygen in the PECOTUR mat were depicted by EDX images. According to SEM and EDX images of fibers, no beads or CDs agglomerates were observed, indicating that nanoparticles are well dispersed in the polymeric matrix.

FTIR spectra of PECOCIT and PECOTUR as well as folic acid, PEC-FA, and PECO were provided in Fig. 5 to demonstrate the chemical composition of CDs-loaded pectin fibers and confirm the presence of CDs through the interactions between polymer matrix and nanostructures. All fibrous mats were analysed by ATIR-FTIR spectroscopy technique while folic acid powder could be assessed by simple FTIR method. Moreover, the sample of folic acid-loaded pectin/PEO fibers was prepared and entitled as PEC-FA accounted for detailed comparison in this test. PECO represents the characteristic peaks of pectin and PEO. For instance, the peaks at 1620, 1739 cm−1 are assigned to CO stretching band of carboxyl groups (COOH) and methyl-esterified carboxyl groups (COOCH3) of pectin, respectively [5,17,45,73]. Additionally, there are the peaks at 1097 and 1016 cm−1 representing the stretching vibrations of COH (side groups) and the COC glycosidic bonds [74,75]. There is a peak at 841 cm−1 induced by the HCH bending vibrations of PEO [5,76]. As shown in the figure, the characteristic peaks of folic acid are observed at 1590 and 1647 cm−1 attributed to CO band in pterin ring (type I amide). Although the peaks in 3000–3700 cm−1 are related to stretching vibrations of OH bonds [77], the peak at 3322 cm−1 induced by NH band in amide can also validates the presence of folic acid [52,78].

The ATIR spectra of PECOCIT and PECOTUR mats displayed the characteristic peaks of pectin, PEO and folic acid but the new peaks were arisen by CDs incorporation. In both samples the peaks in 2889–2985 cm−1 became more intensified which might be due to CH vibrations of aliphatic groups of folic acid [51]. The peaks existed around 1403 and 1640 cm−1 attributed to CO/NH and COOH groups [59]. The peaks at around 2062 cm−1 are also correspond to the stretching vibrations of the compounds containing CN bonds [79]. In PECOTUR spectrum, a peak at 1667 cm−1 is distinctively clear representing the presence of CO bond in NHCO group confirming CDs formation. Furthermore, a peak around 1191 cm−1 could show SC bond [41]. The stretching frequency of carbonyl group at 1739 cm−1 (in PECO) is shifted to 1709 cm−1 (in PECOCIT) and 1712 cm−1 (in PECOTUR) indicating the formation of hydrogen bonds between CDs and polymer matrix [73,80].

Mechanical parameters of PECO, PECOCIT, and PECOTUR such as E, σ (tensile stress at a breakpoint), and ε were calculated using stress-strain curves provided in Table 3 . Although mechanical strength of fibers reduces when their diameters increase [25,46,81], the CDs exhibited high affinity to the Pectin matrix, permitting significant promotion of tensile strength and elastic modulus for the composite fibrous mat in comparison while maintaining comparable extensibility [82]. Likewise, Gobi et al. claimed that the addition of CD onto resin resulted in a 2.25-fold increase in tensile strength compared to the pristine material [83]. CD-loaded fibers exhibited more toughness compared to PECO. Many factors are interfering in the abovementioned mechanical results. For example, the high proportion of composite fibers consists of CDs that are stiffer than the pectin matrix. Besides, the good interfacial adhesion between polymeric matrix and CDs can play a crucial role in mechanical strength improvement [84]. The Nano-scale sizes of CDs leading to a high surface area as well as the functional groups of hydroxyl, carbonyl, and carboxyl on the surfaces of CDs (FACIT and FACITUR) and pectin chains facilitate the interfacial interactions (e.g., hydrogen bonding), which led to better stress distribution in composite fibers [25,85,86]. Notably, according to Matsumoto et al., the shear and electrical field forces of electrospinning together can cause the high orientation of FACITs and FACITURs along the fiber axis, promoting better dispersion and mechanical properties [87–89]. FACITUR is smaller than FACIT, so it has a higher surface area and consequently more interfacial interactions with the pectin matrix. It could be a reason for higher tensile strength and modulus exhibited by the nanocomposite fibers containing FACITUR compared to FACIT-loaded fibers [90]. Surprisingly, the composite mats also represented increased flexibility compared to their unloaded counterpart, also reported by Wang et al. [91] might be due to adequate surface bonding between polymer and CDs. The elongation difference at the breaking point between the PECOCIT and PECOTUR is not statistically significant (P-value > 0.05).

Water uptake is an important characteristic affecting the physicochemical properties of fibers before and after cell seeding [92]. Pectin is naturally hydrophilic and water-soluble without crosslinking due to its hydrophilic functional groups able to considerable hydrogen bonding with water molecules. However, after crosslinking, pectin became a water-insoluble material, and its water uptake degree dramatically reduced [93]. The swelling ratio of three groups of PECO, PECOCIT, and PECOTUR fibers in PBS after 24 h, was provided in Fig. 7. Generally, incorporating FACIT and FACITUR decreases the water uptake of composite fibers compared to PECO. The reasons could be the suitable dispersion of nanoparticles in CDs containing fibers and the strong interactions between carbon nanostructures and polymeric matrix, confirmed by EDX images and FTIR results. From swelling results, the lowest water uptake belonged to PECOTUR (about 26.2 %), indicating the higher surface area and more interfacial interactions of FACITURs with pectin limiting the formation of hydrogen bonds with water molecules. These results are in line with the mechanical properties of PECO, PECOCIT, and PECOTUR. Moreover, adequate water absorption of fibrous mats promotes cell growth and proliferation on them, facilitating nutrients transfer and uptake by cells as well as gradual degradation of fibers which is necessary for tissue engineering [43].

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro folic acid release profile
ce:section.ce:para: Using UV–visible spectrophotometry, a linear calibration curve (R2 = 0.9936) was plotted by measuring the maximum absorbance at 361 nm. The pH of solutions was set at 7.4 and 5.

The release mechanism of folic acid from composite materials is dominantly swelling-controlled rather than Fickian-diffusion-controlled. The swelling behavior of pectin is pH-dependent. As pH increases from 1.4 to 7.4, the swelling rate increases but above pH of 9.4, swelling decreases again [94]. These results can be explained by the apparent pKa (pH at 50 % dissociation) of pectin, which is in the range of 3.55–4.10 depending on the degree of pectin esterification [95]. For pH values higher than pKa of pectin, the pendent COOH groups start disintegrating. Also, with the increase of medium pH, COOH groups in the pectin backbone become ionized by losing protons. As this ionization causes intra-chain repulsive forces, hydrogel pore sizes increase, thereby inducing hydrophilicity and the inward movement of solvent into the hydrogel through diffusion, increasing swelling [96].

The in vitro folic acid release profiles from PECOCIT and PECOTUR samples were studied for 212 h at 37 °C. As shown in Fig. 7, the release of the folic acid from CD-containing fibers was relatively fast during the first 24 h of exposure to PBS, and after that slowed down-converting into a flat line and eventually reached its maximum of 31.34 and 41.16 from PECOCIT and PECOTUR after 212 h (pH = 7.4), respectively. When the pH was set at 5, the cumulative release of folic acid reached about 21 % (the difference was not significant). PECO was used as negative control from which no drug released. A one-hour burst release of CDs containing mats might be due to drugs near the surface of fibers releasing as they absorb water during the first hour. After the primary phase, the drug release behavior of PECOCIT was more controlled than PECOTUR at medium pH of 7.4 because FACIT had fewer carboxyl functional groups on their surface (confirmed by their zeta potential, which was 4 mV while FACITUR's zeta potential was −2.7 mV).

In the case of pH 5, as ionization of pectin is less at pH around its pKa and pectin–drug interaction was weaker, the drug release of folic acid from PECOCIT and PECOTUR did not differ significantly. Therefore, it was observed that the drug release behaviors of the hydrogels are directly proportional to their swelling properties.

In order to evaluate the effect of CDs addition into polymeric matrix on electrical properties of electrospun substrates, electrical conductivity of electrospun mats has been measured and shown in Table 4 . Pectin itself is insulating however, incorporating of CDs into composite fibers resulted in increasing the conductivity of fibers. Polymers with an electrical conductivity of less than 10−5 S m−1 are assumed as insulators [97]. Only the sample of PECOTUR has electrical conductivity greater than 10−5 S m−1 placed within the range of semiconductor materials. There are two methods to increase electrical conductivity in non-conductive polymers during the electrospinning process. One way is coating fibers with conductive materials and the second way is adding conductive constituents to the electrospinning solutions [98]. The presence of FACITUR increases conductivity of the spun nanofibers more than FACITs might be due to their higher structural defects reducing the energy gap between the conduction and the valence band. As the energy of the gap decreases, the electrons move easier from the valence band to the conduction band. The nanostructure concentration and dispersion in polymeric solution also affect the conductivity of fibers effectively [99–101].

Although CDs have also been proven to pose negligible cytotoxicity to many cell lines [102], more investigations are required to completely understand physicochemical interactions between CDs and biomaterials and biomolecules [103]. In this study, in vitro cell viability of U-373 cells on PECO, PECOCIT and PECOTUR was evaluated by MTT assay performed at 1, 5 and 10-day intervals. The results in Fig. 8 illustrate that the composite fibers have the cell survival rate of above 90 % compared to un-treated group (positive control) confirming their high biocompatibility [104,105]. In other studies, the biocompatibility of pectin against varied cell lines was reported repeatedly [106–108]. The in-significant increase in cell viability of PECOTUR compared to positive control could be explained by the presence of folic acid-attached CDs increasing the bioactivity of fibers. Such results are an indication of the potential of CDs-loaded fibers for biomaterial applications.

Fig. 8 also represents the images related to cell attachment of PECO, PECOCIT, and PECOTUR fibers after 24 h incubation. Considering the figure, U-373 cells attachment improves from PECO to PECOCIT and sequentially PECOTUR. The cell on the PECOTUR fibers are well-distributed forming filopodia however the cells on PECO and PECOCIT fibers attached with least contact and did not spread might duo of their higher swelling ratio.

In this study, the nanocomposite Pectin/folic acid-decorated carbon dots nanofibers were successfully developed by electrospinning for the first time. Initially, two CDs consisted of folic acid functional groups on their surfaces were successfully synthesized. The shreds of evidence showed that the average diameters of N, S-doped CD and N-doped CD were 2.17 and 2.74 nm and their conductivities were 71.3 and 3.95 μs, respectively. After incorporating CDs into pectin nanofibers, the electrospun nanofibers' morphology, mechanical, and chemical properties were extensively studied. The SEM/EDS analysis showed a homogeneous size distribution of CDs in nanofibers. By embedding CDs into pectin fibers, the tensile mechanical behavior of nanocomposite fibers was improved. The aforementioned fabricated nanocomposite fibers exhibited enhanced conductivity and biocompatibility of U-373 cells. The release behavior of folic acid from this nanofibrous structure was also assessed at pH 5 and 7.4, and it was revealed that there were sustained releases after approximately nine days. Drug release at pH 7.4 is higher than pH 5 because the ionized carboxyl groups on the polymer chains caused repulsion leading to swelling and more drug released. Considering the results, it can be asserted that the pectin/CD composite fibrous structures have suitable properties as traceable and conductive scaffolds, which can be considered promising candidates for animal studies.

the folic acid-functionalized CDs were incorporated into the electrospun pectin/PEO nanofibrous mat for the first time with enhanced mechanical properties and conductivity as well as controlled drug release.
